records.ety: [F. escourge, fr. L. excoriata (sc. scutica) a stripped off (lash or whip), fr. excoriare to strip, to skin. See Excoriate.]

defs: A lash; a strap or cord; especially, a lash used to inflict pain or punishment; an instrument of punishment or discipline; a whip.
quotes: Up to coach then goes The observed maid, takes both the scourge and reins.

defs: Hence, a means of inflicting punishment, vengeance, or suffering; an infliction of affliction; a punishment.
quotes: What scourge for perjury Can this dark monarchy afford false Clarence?

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Scourged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Scourging (?).]

quotes: Is it lawful for you to scourge a . . . Roman?

defs: To punish with severity; to chastise; to afflict, as for sins or faults, and with the purpose of correction.
quotes: Whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth, and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth.
To scourge and impoverish the people.
